segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the PBS logo on a dark blue background, transitioning to the PBS NOVA logo with a stylized brain graphic, all accompanied by gentle, ambient, and slightly mysterious background music. A 3D model of a human brain with glowing purple neural activity then appears over a grid of MRI scans. A female voice states, "The brain is the biggest mystery in science today." This is followed by another female voice explaining that the brain is "responsible for all the facets of our personality, everything we think and everything we feel," concluding, "It makes you, you." The scene shifts to diverse human experiences: a young man walking outdoors with headphones, a woman stretching on a bridge, a saxophonist playing in a dimly lit room, and a man browsing books in a library. A male voice reflects, "A very large fraction of what's happening in my brain, I am not aware of it all." A close-up of feet walking on a railroad track at sunset evokes a sense of journey, as a third female voice poses questions: "But what exactly is going on in your unconscious brain? What part of your brain is really in charge?" The visuals continue with a man working at a desk with a laptop, a woman reading a book with a yellow sticky note, and a hand flipping through pages of a dense academic text discussing consciousness. A second male voice explains, "All day long we're doing unscripted things that we didn't know we would be doing," adding, "Life is not scripted." The scene transitions to a person rock climbing in a rugged canyon, followed by a silhouette of a dancer performing a graceful pose against a vibrant sunset. A third male voice suggests, "Find a word that has some meaning for you." A fourth female voice notes, "So you might think you've made a choice, representation," then adds, "But in the back of your mind you wonder..." This leads to a fifth female voice dramatically asking, "Come on! Was that really me?" The final frames show a man speaking directly to the camera in a studio setting, and a woman in a contemplative pose, touching her face. A sixth female voice observes, "We might feel like we're in control," as a fourth male voice concludes, "This idea that we're in control of our actions seems critical to our sense of identity." The background music continues throughout, maintaining its thoughtful tone.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dynamic, glowing 3D brain model, its blue neural pathways and red cellular structures pulsating as a calm female voice suggests, "brains may have other ideas." This transitions to a close-up of a woman with curly hair, overlaid with a translucent brain graphic, while a deep, authoritative male voice explains the brain's 90 billion neurons and the illusion of a single person inside our skulls. A sense of mystery builds as a hand reaches out to open a door in a dimly lit hallway, accompanied by a male voice illustrating, "For every Pinocchio, there's always someone kind of pulling the strings behind the scenes." Next, a man in a suit gestures while speaking in a library, as a clear, deliberate male voice states, "There can be two separated minds inside one system." A split-screen view shows two hands drawing identical shapes on paper, while an energetic female voice emphasizes that personality and morality, not just motor skills or language, reside in the brain. A historical black-and-white photograph of a man with a tourniquet-like device around his neck, overlaid with a brain diagram, appears as a calm female voice reflects on how "environment and traumatic events change people." The scene intensifies with a woman in a red blazer speaking against a blurred, fiery background, an assertive female voice dramatically asking, "Your brain. Who's in control?" This is followed by a distorted, glitchy image of a brain, as the assertive female voice announces, "Right now, on NOVA." A cosmic animation then reveals the metallic silver NOVA logo emerging from a nebula, accompanied by dramatic orchestral music, before transitioning to funding credits with the white PBS NOVA logo on a blue background, set to soothing, ethereal space-like music with gentle chimes. The final scenes blend domestic life with science: a close-up of a metal bowl with a cork, into which popcorn is poured amidst popping sounds and the distinct pull of the cork, as the initial calm female voice asks, "Have you ever thought that you've made a crystal clear decision?" before being cut off, "I'm just gonna watch two...", as a person in a red shirt walks toward a TV displaying a brain.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a cozy, brick-walled living room where a woman in a pink blazer, holding a blue bowl of popcorn, sits on a checkered couch. She speaks in a casual, slightly self-deprecating tone, remarking, "two episodes tonight. But the next thing you know... Okay, just one more episode," and then, "Actually, it's time to go to bed. Well, I bet everyone else has already finished this season. Wait, why am I still watching this?" as light, whimsical music plays. Her expression shifts as she watches a screen displaying a colorful, abstract DNA helix that transitions into a vibrant 3D model of a human brain with glowing neural pathways, accompanied by a distinct "whoosh" sound. A close-up of a neuron firing and a detailed MRI brain scan superimposed over a human eye follow, as her voice, now more narrative, declares, "Well, of course, the answer lies in your brain." Electronic, scientific music begins with a "ping" as she continues, "Your brain contains multitudes. It's a complex and intricate three-pound piece of matter. But you actually have no awareness of most of the things that are going on inside your brain." The woman, identified as neuroscientist and clinical psychologist Heather Berlin, then walks through a modern office hallway. A brief, surprised "Oh, man" from a male voice and another "whoosh" punctuate her statement, "And I'm on a journey to discover what's really driving the decisions you make." She enters a lab, collaborating with a man on a wooden box experiment, drawing shapes on paper in a split-screen view mirroring their synchronized actions. A different male voice interjects, "No agency at all," as Heather questions, "Who or what is really in control?" The scene shifts to a split-screen showing a person sleeping under a blanket alongside a complex polygraph readout, indicating sleep monitoring. Heather is then seen in a dimly lit lab, observing a man with a red light device, possibly for brain stimulation. Returning to the living room, she speaks directly to the camera with a thoughtful expression, concluding, "There are important unconscious processes in your brain that you're not aware of." The segment ends with extreme close-ups of a human eye and mouth, followed by intricate mechanical gears, symbolizing the inner workings of the mind and body, all while the white PBS NOVA logo with a brain graphic remains consistently in the top left corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the PBS NOVA logo and a close-up of intricate mechanical gears, accompanied by upbeat, slightly mysterious music with a ticking sound, as a female narrator explains that the brain is usually a "coordinated, well-oiled machine." The Woman in Pink Blazer is then seen on a checkered couch, holding a blue bowl of popcorn and gesturing expressively, continuing to narrate about the brain when "things are out of sync." The scene transitions to a serene sunset over mountains with the title 'YOUR BRAIN WHO'S IN CONTROL?' overlaid, then a night sky with a full moon, as the music becomes more intense with faint, ghostly vocalizations. A man with curly hair appears distressed, leaning over a bathroom sink, accompanied by a low, eerie sound and a high-pitched whirring. The Woman in Pink Blazer, now in a black blazer, speaks directly to the camera against a brick background, as the sound of grinding gears and a 'click' is heard. A classic alarm clock is shown on a bedside table, followed by a woman tossing and turning in bed with 'EDITED BY Ryan Shepheard' text, as the narrator introduces something done daily with "little to no conscious control." A man with wide-open eyes lies in bed in a dimly lit room with 'HOSTED BY Heather Berlin' text, as the narrator states this activity is "Sleeping." A night-vision shot shows a man suddenly sitting up in bed, screaming (with on-screen text capturing his vocalizations), accompanied by dramatic, suspenseful music, a deep, guttural sound, and a faint, high-pitched whine, as the narrator explains that for some, sleep is not always at rest, and introduces sleepwalking. Finally, Emmanuel During, MD, identified by on-screen text, speaks in an office setting, stating in a male voice with a slight accent that "Sleepwalking is a glitch in the system."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a black-and-white kitchen where a man in a white long-sleeve shirt drinks from a glass, accompanied by the sounds of pouring liquid and clinking, as a male voice states, "our identity is not in control" and recounts patients saying, "I didn't do that, that's not possible." The scene transitions to color, showing the same man, now in a black tank top, opening a refrigerator and then washing dishes at the sink, with the sounds of dishwashing audible. On-screen credits for script, story, production, and direction appear. The male voice continues, now identified as a man in a dark suit speaking directly to the camera, explaining that "sleepwalking" is a "very common condition" where "you sleep, but during your sleep, you will walk," all set to a gentle background music. The scene then shifts to a humanoid robot with exposed mechanical joints, navigating an outdoor obstacle course with orange barriers, accompanied by mechanical whirring and thudding sounds, as the male voice describes the complexity of "teaching a robot all the inputs and outputs for a body to move forward on two legs without falling." The robot then walks inside a warehouse. Following this, a woman with long red hair is seen hiking on a dirt trail through dry brush under a clear blue sky, while a different female voice asks, "How is it possible to do complex behaviors like walking, eating, and sometimes even driving while sleeping?" The segment concludes with a woman in a black coat walking on a rainy city sidewalk with an umbrella, entering the 'Mount Sinai Union Square Care Center' through glass doors, with reflections of the wet street visible.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the PBS NOVA logo consistently displayed in the top left corner, as a woman in a black coat, identified as the narrator, walks through a modern, well-lit office hallway with glass walls, entering a 'Mount Sinai Union Square Care Center.' Upbeat, slightly mysterious background music plays as the narrator's voice states, "To find out, I'm visiting a sleep center at the Icon School of Medicine at Mount Sinai." She then meets with another woman in a black top, seated at a wooden desk adorned with a 3D brain model, a tablet, and office supplies. Their serious conversation begins with the woman in the black coat asking, "So tell me a little bit about what's happening with you at night and your sleepwalking." The woman in the black top, a patient, responds, describing "weird things" she's done, like painting "a triangle, a perfect triangle" in her kitchen, and laughing as she recounts finding her sleepwalking creations. The camera alternates between close-ups of their expressive faces and wider shots of the office, featuring plants, computers, and a corkboard. The scene transitions to the woman in the black coat walking down a hospital corridor with a man in a suit, as the narrator's voice explains that "Emmanuel Mignot is studying what's going on in the brain when someone sleepwalks." The pair then enters a sleep lab where a patient, referred to by the narrator as being "wired up with sensors," is being fitted with EEG electrodes by a technician in a lab coat. The patient lies in bed, connected to monitoring equipment, while the technician precisely adjusts the setup. The video then cuts to a control room where two individuals analyze sleep data on large screens displaying complex waveforms, including EEG, ECG, and muscle activity traces. The woman in the black coat asks about the "blue lines" on the screen, and a male expert's voice identifies them as "eye movements," then the "black lines" as "brainwaves." A split-screen view shows the real-time data alongside a night-vision camera feed of the sleeping patient, highlighting physiological changes during sleep stages. The expert's voice explains that "this patient obviously is lying in bed... dozes off slowly, feels sleepy, and as we move on, he dives into deep sleep." The segment concludes with a close-up of the sleep monitoring screen, emphasizing the detailed polygraph readout with labels such as 'NREM 3' and 'Chin 2-Chin 1,' indicating deep sleep and muscle tone monitoring, directly correlating with the expert's explanation.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a polygraph readout screen, displaying multiple colored waveforms for EEG, ECG, and muscle activity, labeled with technical annotations like 'NREM 3' and 'Chin 2-Chin 1'. A female narrator explains that "During sleep, your brain cycles through phases of high and low activity," and when "brain waves slow down, scientists call this deep sleep." The scene transitions to a dark, blue-toned night-vision shot of a person sleeping, overlaid with a yellow waveform and a dashed line marking the onset of 'DEEP SLEEP'. The narrator then adds, "But when someone sleepwalks..." The video cuts to a control room where a male scientist in a dark suit and light blue tie (Man in Suit) and a female scientist with long dark hair in a black coat (Woman in Black Coat) are seated, observing a large sleep monitoring screen. The Man in Suit analyzes the data, stating, "First of all, everything looks good. You see the brain waves... Everything is very, very monotonous, sort of slow waves." The screen shows a split view: the left side displays the polygraph readout with fluctuating waveforms, while the right side shows a night-vision camera feed of a person in bed. The Man in Suit continues, noting "there's a build-up of slow wave that the amplitude goes up, then then suddenly..." as a sharp electronic tone sounds. The person in bed suddenly sits up, appearing agitated, and then begins to physically interact with another person lying beside them, who is also wearing EEG electrodes. The Woman in Black Coat reacts with a surprised "Wow," and the Man in Suit observes, "So he's seemingly awake, sudden. Looks like a sudden arousal." The Woman in Black Coat adds, "Looks sort of scared," to which the Man in Suit responds, "Very brisk, very fast, eyes open." He then points out, "There's a sort of a split then." The female narrator explains, "The patient looks like they're awake, but a couple of key brain regions seem to stay asleep." The Man in Suit elaborates, "There's part of the brain stays in slow wave sleep, it's such a deep stage of sleep, it's hard to wake up, and the other part of the brain is already awake." The segment concludes with a stylized 3D brain model, highlighting the 'PREFRONTAL CORTEX' in red, as the narrator begins to say, "One part of the brain..." The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout, accompanied by a subtle, scientific-sounding ambient background music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a stylized 3D brain model, highlighting the 'PREFRONTAL CORTEX' and 'POSTERIOR PARIETAL CORTEX' in red against a dark background, with the PBS NOVA logo in the top left. A female narrator explains that the prefrontal cortex is the region that doesn't wake up during sleepwalking and is responsible for deliberate choices and self-awareness. The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit and light blue tie, holding a realistic brain model and gesturing in a lab-like setting, who, speaking with an accent, identifies the prefrontal cortex as 'the decision maker' and explains that many brain parts can be engaged without conscious awareness. The video then cuts to a kitchen scene where a woman in a pink floral dress walks toward the stove, stumbles erratically, and appears disoriented and uncoordinated. This visual is immediately followed by another brain diagram, this time highlighting 'MOTOR CORTEX', 'VISUAL CORTEX', 'LANGUAGE', and 'BALANCE' regions in yellow, as the female narrator explains that during sleepwalking, these areas can become active without engaging the prefrontal cortex. The disoriented woman is shown again in the kitchen, and a male voice is heard asking, 'And what exactly are you doing, ma'am?' to which a female voice replies, 'It's a special code.' The final shot introduces Susana Martinez-Conde, PhD, from SUNY Downstate Health Sciences University, speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit office, while the female narrator concludes that sleepwalking experiences reveal consciousness is not an 'all or none' situation, and our unconscious makes a lot of everyday actions possible.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with a close-up of a woman in a bright yellow hoodie, standing outdoors at dusk, speaking expressively about 'decisions for us.' This transitions to Nancy Kanwisher, PhD from MIT McGovern Institute, who, with a stylized 3D brain model glowing beside her, explains in a clear female voice how the brain handles 'boring stuff' like regulating heart rate, temperature, and digestion, along with 'motor function, sensory function, motor sensory integration, memory representation,' all happening 'below the surface.' The 3D brain model then floats in darkness, pulsing with light, accompanied by a deep, guttural 'Ooh, ooh, ooh, ooh, ooh' sound and a 'whoosh' effect. Daniela Schiller, PhD from Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, then appears, calmly explaining how during sleepwalking, brain regions controlling movement, vision, and breathing can cause 'mischief without you even knowing it,' and how these regions 'check out' during anesthesia. The scene shifts to Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD from the University of Chicago, who, with expressive hand gestures, states that anesthetics can 'remove your conscious experience.' Finally, Anil Seth, PhD from the University of Sussex, speaks directly to the camera, gesturing as he discusses how 'consciousness comes in degrees,' explaining that we can lose consciousness in sleep, but 'in a more profound way' under general anesthesia. Throughout, the PBS NOVA logo is visible, and on-screen text identifies each speaker and their affiliation.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a PBS NOVA logo as a faint, sustained electronic hum begins. A female narrator recounts, "When I was a young researcher working in anesthesiology, I saw this firsthand," then asks, "So what happens to your brain activity when you go under?" as a woman in a white lab coat and glasses walks down a dimly lit hospital corridor with a serious expression. An electronic whoosh transitions the scene to a patient lying on an operating table, receiving a nasal spray from a medical professional in blue gloves, while sustained, slightly ominous electronic music with a rhythmic pulse plays. A close-up shows a surgeon in full surgical attire under a bright surgical light. The woman from the beginning is then seen putting on a surgical mask and cap, preparing for the procedure, before walking down a brightly lit hallway marked 'Lunder 3 Operating Rooms 61-73' to join a team of medical staff in blue scrubs. The narrator continues, "Neuroscientist Emery Brown is measuring the line that separates being conscious from being unconscious." The team enters an operating room where the patient is prepped, with electrodes placed on their head and an IV bag labeled 'Phenylephrine' being hung. A medical monitor displays a heart rate of 93 bpm. Emery N. Brown, MD, PhD, identified by on-screen text, speaks in an interview setting, stating, "I want to guarantee my patients that when I say you're unconscious, you're not going to perceive pain. You won't be moving around. You won't remember anything that's occurring. Your heart rate and blood pressure and other physiological systems will be well controlled." The electronic music continues as the team administers anesthesia, with a Baxter infusion pump showing 'Propofol' delivered at 120 mL/hr. A faint voice from the medical staff is heard saying "What else?". The narrator explains, "The patient is undergoing surgery. But before the surgeons can operate, the anesthesiologists have to put her under, render her unconscious with special drugs." The patient is then intubated, and a female medical professional tells the patient, "I'm starting to give you medicines that might make you feel kind of drowsy." A male medical professional instructs, "Look straight ahead. Look straight ahead," as the medical team continues to monitor and adjust equipment, ensuring the patient is stable under anesthesia, capturing the precision, coordination, and high-stakes environment of the surgical procedure.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a brightly lit operating room where a medical team in blue scrubs, surgical caps, and masks prepares a patient. A male surgeon with glasses and a gray beard directs the patient, asking "Are we expecting to move?" and noting "So you're moving her head but her eyes stay straight." He then instructs, "All right, we're gonna have you breathe." The patient receives a nasal spray, followed by the placement of an oxygen mask and manual ventilation using a blue bag-valve mask, accompanied by monitor beeps. The surgeon asks, "Can you see my finger here? Follow with your eyes," and then, "If you can't follow anymore, tell me, all right?" He continues to check, asking "Can you hear me?" A female medical professional observes, "See her eyes are fixed now," to which the surgeon confirms, "Yeah." The surgeon then notes, "Is it the EEG has a large slow oscillation, see there?" and the female professional responds, "Yeah, yeah." The surgeon states, "Her brainstem is out," which she confirms with "That's out. That's out." The scene shifts to a Masimo monitor displaying vital signs, including a brain signal reading dropping from 85 to 42. A female narrator's voice, accompanied by a tense, atmospheric musical score, explains, "When you go under, it can feel like one second you're here, and the next, you're out. What's going on in the brain when this happens?" The team attaches EEG electrodes, labeled 'F2' and 'C2,' to the patient’s scalp, and an EEG monitor shows a flatline. The narrator continues, "Emery uses a device called an EEG. A set of electrodes that rests on the scalp and detects electrical activity in the brain. That activity comes in the form of waves." The video transitions to a graphic illustrating 'ACTIVE,' 'RELAXED,' and 'REM SLEEP' brain wave patterns. A male voice, likely the surgeon, concludes, "The brain generates brain waves or oscillations." The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a blackboard-style graphic illustrating brain wave patterns labeled 'ACTIVE,' 'RELAXED,' 'REM SLEEP,' and 'DEEP SLEEP,' each represented by distinct yellow waveforms. A clear female narrator's voice explains that these brain waves are measured by their frequency—how fast they come and go—and their amplitude—how small or big they are—as the video zooms in on a single waveform to highlight these properties. A male voice, later identified as Emery N. Brown, MD, PhD, begins discussing the oscillations typically seen when someone is conscious, stating that an awake EEG shows a "very rich response" which "goes away" when anesthetized. The scene transitions to a real-world medical setting, showing two Masimo monitors in an operating room displaying vital signs, including heart rates of 89 and 29, blood pressure, and brain wave activity. Dr. Brown, a bald man with a gray beard and glasses, is then shown speaking in an interview, further explaining that the difference between conscious and unconscious states represents a transition. He points out how oscillations are "really big" in a conscious state, contrasting with a female interviewer's comment about them being "just sort of little" before. As Dr. Brown speaks, a brain wave graphic overlays a split screen, visually demarcating 'CONSCIOUS' and 'UNCONSCIOUS' states. The video then shows a group of friends laughing and interacting in a bright, cozy living room, accompanied by their background chatter and laughter, with a yellow brain wave graphic superimposed, symbolizing active consciousness. The narrator's voice returns, describing how awake and fully aware brain activity is "diverse and dynamic," like an "exciting conversation." This vibrant scene then shifts to a blurred operating room with medical staff in blue scrubs and masks, as the brain wave graphic declines, symbolizing unconsciousness, accompanied by a subtle whooshing sound. The narrator explains that when "anesthesia drugs hit the brain," activity is "dramatically reduced to dull, slow, rolling brain waves," and the "once dynamic conversation becomes an unintelligible hum." The video returns to the blurred friends, with the brain wave continuing to dip, reinforcing the contrast, before the friends' scene fades into a blurred, dreamlike state, with the brain wave pattern becoming irregular and subdued, visually representing the loss of consciousness.
segments.visual_original_caption: The segment begins with a blackboard-style graphic illustrating brain wave patterns labeled 'ACTIVE,' 'RELAXED,' 'REM SLEEP,' and 'DEEP SLEEP,' each represented by distinct yellow waveforms. The video then zooms in on a single waveform, highlighting 'FREQUENCY' with a horizontal arrow and 'AMPLITUDE' with a vertical arrow, visually explaining the properties of brain waves. The scene transitions to a real-world medical setting, showing two Masimo monitors in an operating room, displaying vital signs including heart rate, blood pressure, and brain wave activity, with one monitor showing a heart rate of 89 and the other 29. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Emery N. Brown, MD, PhD, a bald man with a gray beard and glasses, wearing a blue button-down shirt, speaking in an interview setting. He gestures as he explains in a clear voice how altering the brain's communication can make someone unconscious, stating, "If you alter how the parts of the brain communicate sufficiently, you can make someone unconscious." He continues, "So that's what the drugs are doing. They're altering the way the various parts of the brain communicate." The scene transitions to an operating room with a patient under anesthesia, where a female narrator's voice introduces a specific brain region, explaining, "There's one region of the brain in particular that acts as a communication hub." A stylized black-and-white brain diagram with a glowing yellow 'THALAMUS' appears as the narrator names it, "The thalamus." She describes it further: "It's made up of two parts, each about the size of a walnut, and sits deep inside your brain." Emery N. Brown returns, elaborating on the thalamus's role: "Thalamus is a central way station for all sorts of information processing. Auditory information goes through there, visual information goes through there, pain information goes through there." He emphasizes its importance, adding, "If I could take out just one brain center to make you unconscious, it would probably be the thalamus because it's such a central actor in processing all types of information." The video then shows a woman in a black top touching her wrist. The operating room scene resumes with medical staff in blue scrubs. The female narrator explains, "After a couple of hours of surgery, the medical team is tapering off the anesthesia drugs." Soft, ambient music begins. A Masimo monitor displays the patient's heart rate dropping to 66 bpm, with on-screen text reading, 'She started to take some breaths on her own.' The EEG monitor shows brain wave activity, with a yellow waveform transitioning from 'ACTIVE' to 'RELAXED' and 'DEEP SLEEP.' The narrator's voice continues, "And the EEG reveals the patient's brainwave activity becoming more complex as she wakes up." Emery N. Brown is briefly seen again, starting to say, "She started to take..." before the segment ends.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a sterile operating room where a patient, covered by a blue drape, lies on a surgical table. Medical professionals in blue scrubs and masks prepare for a procedure, with close-ups showing the patient’s hand with an IV line and pulse oximeter. A surgeon with a gray beard and glasses, wearing blue scrubs and a surgical cap, oversees the team, instructing the patient in a calm male voice to "open your eyes wide" and "squeeze my hand." The camera pans to a monitor displaying vital signs, including a heart rate of 89, then to a table covered with brain MRI scans where a medical professional points to a specific area. A medical professional adjusts an IV bag labeled 'Phenylephrine', followed by a close-up of an IV drip. During these scenes, a male voice, later identified as Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD, begins to explain that "Consciousness is really having active cognitive processing, being able to think and act," while the surgeon's voice interjects, confirming "Surgeries all done, okay?" Kasthuri continues, describing consciousness as "the integration of that information, which allows us to start to understand how consciousness is actually formed." Upbeat, slightly dramatic orchestral music begins as the scene transitions to Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD, speaking expressively in a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls, continuing his explanation that "Consciousness can obviously interact with the physical world like we can. We can use drugs to remove it, we go to sleep and we're not conscious, and yet it's tenuous at the same time. We can't say how any specific set of neurons working together produces consciousness." The music continues subtly. The scene then shifts to Rebecca Saxe, PhD, from MIT, seated in a modern office with large windows, gesturing as she speaks in a clear female voice, stating, "It's so clear that anesthesia is some kind of change of consciousness, right? The whole brain is there, the pieces are there, but the messages aren't getting through in a way that makes for a conscious experience." A translucent 3D brain model appears beside her, then floats in darkness, glowing with purple and orange neural networks, accompanied by a distinct, almost electronic "drip" sound effect. The video returns to the operating room, showing a Masimo monitor with a heart rate of 65 and brain wave activity, as a hand adjusts the screen, while Rebecca Saxe concludes, "And that's the difference between being aware and not being aware. So the level of con..." The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Heather Berlin, PhD, MPH, standing in front of a brick wall, speaking expressively with hand gestures, wearing a black blazer over a dark, sparkly top. She articulately explains that communication among brain regions is the key difference between being conscious and unconscious, and that no single area is responsible for consciousness, but rather the communication itself. As she speaks, the scene transitions to a stylized 3D animation of a neural network, with glowing red and pink filaments dynamically moving and connecting, accompanied by light background music. A distinct 'whoosh' sound marks a transition, and an unidentified male voice begins to speak, explaining that the left hand is governed by the right hemisphere, as a silhouette of a bald man with a beard gazes contemplatively out a window at a sunset over a mountainous landscape. Heather Berlin then reappears, now in a room with light blue paneled walls, continuing her narration with animated gestures, describing how people who have undergone split-brain surgery can't really communicate between brain halves. This is shown visually with a sepia-toned close-up of hands arranging wooden blocks on a table, suggesting a cognitive task, with a small notebook nearby. Heather continues, stating it's as if they have two minds in a single brain, punctuated by a dramatic sound effect. The unidentified male voice from earlier returns, posing a question about what happens when both hands try to solve a problem together, noting they fight over each other, as the block arrangement is shown again, this time with a text overlay promoting the PBS App. The segment concludes with Michael Miller, PhD, from the University of California, Santa Barbara, holding a detailed model of a human brain and gesturing toward its structures, explaining in a calm, authoritative voice that the human brain contains two sides, the left and right hemispheres, connected by a big bundle of fibers called the corpus callosum, with a bookshelf in the background. The white PBS NOVA logo with a brain graphic remains consistently in the top left corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michael Miller, PhD, holding a detailed anatomical brain model, explaining in a clear, explanatory tone that "All the communication from one side of the brain to the other has to pass through this fiber bundle." This transitions to a stylized 3D animation of a brain, splitting into two hemispheres connected by a highlighted yellow structure labeled 'CORPUS CALLOSUM.' A female narrator explains that "For some people with epilepsy, a seizure in one hemisphere can quickly spread to the other by way of the corpus callosum," and that "if that bridge is surgically severed, a seizure can no longer cross to the other side of the brain." The animation then shows the corpus callosum being severed, symbolizing a split-brain condition. The narrator continues, noting these surgeries led to "astounding research into how the two hemispheres function." Archival footage from September 25, 1983, shows a person wearing a specialized eye-tracking device and hands performing a task, accompanied by an archival male voice instructing, "With your left hand, make me the A-OK sign," followed by laughter and indistinct speech. The narrator then states she "met two pioneers in the field," naming "Michael Miller and Michael Gazzaniga," before cutting to a modern lab setting. Here, Michael Miller, along with a woman in a beige cardigan (addressed as Heather) and another in a black top, interacts around a wooden experimental apparatus. A lab monitor displays a 'Welcome' screen. Michael Miller gestures, explaining the procedure to Heather, saying, "So Heather, what you're going to see are two shapes," as the narrator explains he asked her to "step into his lab to do a few simple tests." The white PBS NOVA logo with a brain graphic is consistently visible in the top left corner throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern laboratory where Michael Miller, PhD, a man with a gray beard and curly hair wearing a light blue button-down shirt, instructs two women on a cognitive experiment. He calmly explains, "You'll draw the shape on the left side of the screen with your left hand and the shape on the right side of the screen with your right hand," emphasizing drawing "as quickly as you can at the same time." The woman in a beige cardigan and glasses, seated at a wooden experimental apparatus designed to isolate visual input to each brain hemisphere, confirms with an "Okay," while the woman in a black top playfully remarks, "Piece of cake, right?" A monitor displays simple geometric shapes—a square and a circle—on a light blue background for the participant to draw. As the woman in beige draws with both hands, producing two distinct, often asymmetrical shapes, Michael Miller observes, chuckling, "I'm not sure what you're drawing over here," to which she responds with an "Oh" and humorously adds, "I mentioned I didn't get that much sleep last night." A calm, informative narrator's voice then explains, "The left side of the brain controls most of the right side of the body, and the right side of the brain controls most of the left side of the body," over a soft, educational score. The woman in beige reflects on her drawing process, noting how she starts with different intentions but her hands "just sort of like sync up together," prompting Michael Miller's affirmative "Yeah, yeah." The woman in black top playfully encourages, "Come on, man." Michael Miller then reassures, "It's perfectly normal," beginning to explain the motor commands in each hemisphere. The scene, featuring alternating close-ups of the hands drawing and wider shots of the participants, highlights the split-brain phenomenon, with on-screen text labeling 'LEFT BRAIN' and 'RIGHT BRAIN', all under the PBS NOVA logo.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment opens in a modern laboratory setting where Michael Miller, PhD, a man with a gray beard and curly hair wearing a light blue button-down shirt, is conducting a cognitive experiment with two women. One woman, wearing a beige cardigan and glasses, is seated at a wooden experimental apparatus with a split-screen setup, while the other, in a black top, observes from the side. The apparatus features a wooden box with two openings, each with a notebook and pen, designed to isolate visual input to each hemisphere of the brain. A monitor displays simple geometric shapes—a square and a circle—on a light blue background, which the participant is instructed to draw. As the experiment progresses, the woman in the beige cardigan draws with her right hand (controlled by the left brain) and her left hand (controlled by the right brain), producing two distinct, often asymmetrical shapes. The camera alternates between close-ups of the hands drawing and wider shots of the participants interacting. On-screen text labels the left and right sides of the split-screen as 'LEFT BRAIN' and 'RIGHT BRAIN' to emphasize the hemispheric division. Michael Miller gestures and explains the process, while the women react with smiles and curiosity. The environment is well-lit, with a laptop, water bottle, and cables visible in the background, reinforcing the scientific context. The segment highlights the split-brain phenomenon, demonstrating how each hemisphere processes visual information independently, with the right brain often producing more abstract or creative interpretations, as seen in the differing drawings.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a woman in a beige cardigan and glasses, seated at a wooden experimental apparatus, drawing with both hands on separate notebooks. A male voice, identified as Michael Gazzaniga, explains the concept of interfering with motor commands in the other hemisphere. The camera zooms in on her notebooks, revealing two distinct, asymmetrical shapes being drawn simultaneously, as a female narrator's voice describes the near impossibility for her to force her hands to draw two different things at the same time. The scene transitions to 'JOE,' a split-brain patient, performing the same task, drawing different shapes on separate papers while focused on a blank blue computer monitor. The narrator elaborates that for someone whose two hemispheres are disconnected, there's no interference, as if one mind controls each hand. She continues, explaining that the split isn't just movement, but also visual fields. A black-and-white animated diagram of the brain then illustrates how only half of the visual field goes to each side of the brain, with green and red lines tracing neural pathways. Michael Gazzaniga's voice further clarifies that everything to the left visual field goes only to the right hemisphere, and vice-versa. The video briefly shows a woman on a landline phone in an office and a close-up of a hand holding a Rubik’s cube, before returning to Michael Gazzaniga, PhD, seated at a desk with a brain model. He continues his academic commentary, explaining that the left part of the brain houses language and speech centers, enabling communication, while the right side is very important in the evaluation of emotions.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michael Gazzaniga, PhD, an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue zip-up sweater, seated at a wooden desk in a well-lit academic setting with bookshelves in the background. He gestures with both hands raised as if to emphasize a point, speaking in a calm, academic tone about the "ation of visual space," with a 3D brain model on the desk before him. He then states, "I'm going to give you a test." The scene cuts to grainy archival footage of a woman in a light blue shirt, speaking with a clear, instructional voice, telling a participant in a white lab coat, "If you look right at my nose, and I'm going to hold up my hands. You tell me how many fingers you see, all right?" The video then returns to Gazzaniga, who asks, "How many fingers do you see?" He illustrates by holding up one finger on each hand, explaining, "You see two, right? Why did you see two? This one went to your left hemisphere. This one went to your right hemisphere, way over on the other side of your brain." An animated diagram appears, showing the left and right visual fields with corresponding brain hemispheres highlighted in red and green, and the optic nerves crossing. The animation then highlights the corpus callosum in yellow, as Gazzaniga's voice explains, "How did your left hemisphere know about it? That pathway, the corpus callosum. It transfers that information." The scene cuts back to Gazzaniga, who continues his explanation with the same hand gesture, stating, "Now I'm going to split your brain. And I do the same test. How many fingers do I see?" The archival footage resumes, showing a close-up of hands, and the participant's slightly muffled female voice responds, "Two." On-screen text 'You see anything else?' appears as the instructional woman's voice asks, "You see anything else?" The participant's voice responds, "No, no," and the instructional woman's voice confirms, "Okay." The segment concludes with Gazzaniga, followed by the animated diagram reappearing, now showing a speech bubble icon near the left hemisphere, indicating language processing, and a dashed line through the corpus callosum, symbolizing its severance in split-brain patients, as Gazzaniga explains, "You see one, you see this one because that goes straight to your left talking hemisphere. This one is still going to your right hemisphere, which has now been disconnected." The white PBS NOVA logo with a brain graphic remains consistently in the top left corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Michael Gazzaniga, PhD, an older man with white hair and glasses, seated at a wooden desk with a 3D brain model, gesturing as he explains the split-brain concept. His voice clearly articulates how the left brain cannot communicate what the right brain perceives after the 'highway' of information has been cut, stating, "So your left brain can't talk about this... even though your right brain is seeing this finger. It just can't talk about it because the highway that communicates that information has been cut." This explanation is visually reinforced by an animated brain diagram illustrating left and right visual fields, optic nerve pathways, and hemispheres, with a yellow corpus callosum shown being severed, and a speech bubble near the left hemisphere. The scene cuts back to Gazzaniga, continuing his expressive hand movements, before transitioning to grainy archival footage. Here, a woman in a light blue shirt instructs a participant in a white lab coat, with on-screen text prompting, 'Show me with your right hand what you see' and 'Show me with your left hand what you see,' accompanied by the instructor's clear voice and the participant's verbal responses of "Okay" and "One," demonstrating a split-brain experiment. A subtle, investigative background music plays throughout. The video then shifts to a modern lab setting where Michael Miller, PhD, a man with a gray beard and curly hair, is seated at a wooden experimental apparatus with a participant. His voice describes the "most remarkable thing to witness" – how a "whole other entity in the head" can function "completely separate from the other side." A computer monitor displays a blue screen with a central crosshair, then briefly shows a face, indicating visual stimuli, with a close-up of the monitor showing the face as the participant observes it. A female narrator's voice provides further context, explaining that "researchers conducted tests to explore how a split-brain patient's two hemispheres work independently from one another," including "a now famous experiment of a patient named Joe." The white PBS NOVA logo with a brain graphic is consistently displayed in the top left corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with grainy, vintage-style split-screen footage, showing a male participant in a light-colored shirt intently focused and touching his chin in the top frame, while the bottom frame shows the back of another person’s head. The PBS NOVA logo is visible. A male experimenter's voice is heard saying "right and dot." A female narrator explains the experiment, detailing how quickly flashing a word to the left visual field sends it exclusively to the right, non-verbal half of the brain. Michael Gazzaniga, PhD, then explains in his clear, authoritative voice that the only way to know if the word registered is if the participant can write something out. The Woman in Beige Cardigan asks if this is with the hand controlled by his right hemisphere, to which Gazzaniga confirms it's his left hand. The screen then displays 'TEXAS +' as the Woman in Beige Cardigan states they flashed the word "Texas." The vintage footage returns, showing the man looking down thoughtfully, accompanied by experimenters' voices giving instructions like "See Nick" and "and flash, see the word." The scene transitions to a modern interview setting where Michael Gazzaniga, an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue zip-up sweater, sits at a table with the Woman in Beige Cardigan, with a bookshelf in the background. She explains that his right hemisphere is seeing it, and Gazzaniga clarifies that while the right hemisphere sees it, it cannot talk. The Woman in Beige Cardigan acknowledges this. The video cuts back to the yellow-green tinted vintage footage, where the man in the top frame begins drawing a simple shape on a whiteboard with a marker, following an experimenter's instruction to "draw for me that thing upside down." The Woman in Beige Cardigan then comments that the participant claims not to have seen anything, which Gazzaniga confirms. The segment alternates between the interview, where Gazzaniga speaks and the woman listens attentively, occasionally smiling, and the vintage footage, where the man continues to draw a more defined, cloud-like shape. The Woman in Beige Cardigan expresses surprise, exclaiming, "Oh my god! Wow! He was able to do Texas upside down!"

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a modern interview setting with Michael Gazzaniga, PhD, an older man with white hair and glasses, explaining in a clear, authoritative voice how a split-brain patient had no idea what he was drawing. This explanation is intercut with grainy, green-tinted archival footage of JOE, the split-brain patient, drawing with both hands on separate notebooks within a wooden experimental apparatus, his focused face visible in a small inset. JOE's voice is heard directly stating, "I can't tell what it is." Back in the interview, the Woman in Beige Cardigan, wearing glasses and a beige cardigan, reacts with an amazed "Wow," while the Man in Blue Shirt, with a gray beard, smiles and nods. Dr. Gazzaniga continues to detail the experiment, describing how they later showed the word again. A female narrator's voice then provides context, explaining that the word "Texas" was shown to JOE's right non-verbal hemisphere. When asked what he saw, JOE's voice reveals his left hemisphere's confusion: "I'm aware of a word, I just didn't see what it was." Dr. Gazzaniga then instructs JOE to "Draw something that goes with that. A symbol of that." The archival footage shows JOE sketching a crown-like form, prompting the Woman in Beige Cardigan to exclaim, "Oh wow. So he draws a cowboy hat." Dr. Gazzaniga clarifies that JOE's right hemisphere knew exactly what he was drawing, but his left was still confused, a point the Woman in Beige Cardigan affirms with "Right." Dr. Gazzaniga asks, "What's that?" and JOE's voice replies, "Cowboy hat." Dr. Gazzaniga repeats, "Cowboy hat?" and then asks, "What was the word?" JOE answers, "Texas," which the Woman in Beige Cardigan echoes with a surprised "Texas." Dr. Gazzaniga presses, "Did you see Texas?" to which JOE replies, "No." The segment concludes with the female narrator's voice summarizing, "The split-brain phenomenon," all while a subtle, thoughtful background music plays and the PBS NOVA logo remains consistently displayed in the top left corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with grainy, green-tinted archival footage of JOE, a split-brain patient, seated at a wooden experimental apparatus, drawing with both hands on separate notebooks, his focused face visible in a small inset. A male voice, later identified as Michael Gazzaniga, explains that there can be 'two separated minds, if you will, inside of a skull.' The scene transitions to Michael Gazzaniga, PhD, an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark blue zip-up sweater, seated at a table with a 3D brain model, gesturing expressively as he continues, 'The cooperation is on the paper, not inside the head. It's an astounding example of cross-cueing and management of two mental systems into one unified act.' The video cuts back to JOE continuing his drawing, then returns to Gazzaniga, who adds, 'And the idea is, maybe that's going on in us all the time too.' The scene shifts to a woman in a striped shirt, the Woman Stretching, leaning on a blue railing outdoors with eyes closed, appearing contemplative, as a female voice, later identified as Nancy Kanwisher, begins to speak. This is followed by 3D animated brain models highlighting the cerebellum in red, the occipital lobe in blue, and the parietal lobe in green, each labeled on-screen. Nancy Kanwisher, PhD, a woman with short gray hair and blue glasses, wearing a blue t-shirt, is then seen seated in a modern, well-lit room, speaking with expressive hand gestures, explaining, 'Each of us has a sense that we're a unitary being, but actually that belies the fact that each of us, each of our minds, is actually composed of lots of different pieces that are doing different things.' She continues, 'And so a search for 'where am I' in all of this is a little bit misguided because the 'I' is not such a unitary thing in the first place.' The video transitions to a dynamic 3D animation of a neural network with glowing orange filaments, representing active neurons. Finally, Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD, a man with curly gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark floral shirt, appears in a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls, speaking expressively, stating, 'That feeling of unity of me is actually distributed across almost 90 billion neurons. This illusion that there's a single person inside...' The PBS NOVA logo is consistently displayed in the top left corner throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, slightly mysterious background music as a vibrant, rotating 3D brain model, rendered in thermal colors, appears against a black background, with the PBS NOVA logo in the top left. A male voice states, 'our skulls.' A female narrator then explains, 'Inside your brain are over a hundred distinct regions,' as the scene shifts to a low-angle shot of a person’s legs walking on a tennis court, holding a racket, followed by a close-up of a human eye with a warm, sunlit glow. The narrative continues with a group of young people interacting under an overpass, backlit by sunlight, creating golden silhouettes, while the narrator details how 'Many different systems in the brain control what you do from movement to vision to speech, and even social interaction.' A woman with curly hair in a dark jacket walks into a room and sits, transitioning to an interview with Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD from Harvard University, who, speaking expressively in a softly lit room with colorful art supplies, asserts, 'I think most human beings like to believe that their mind is under their own control... that everything I do has been chosen by me. And if there is a story from the brain to tell, it is that we are quite wrong.' The scene cuts to a woman driving a car, her hands on the steering wheel, then her focused reflection in the rearview mirror, before returning to Banaji. A close-up of a young man laughing with a friend under the overpass is shown, followed by a man and woman walking along a waterfront with a city skyline, and an overhead shot of a bustling crowd in a sunlit plaza with elongated shadows. The female narrator returns, explaining, 'Not only are there multiple parts of your brain influencing you, but there are things in the world around you that influence your brain, including other people.' The segment concludes with interviews: first, Rebecca Saxe, PhD from MIT, a woman with curly light brown hair and glasses, states, 'How we act and who we are in our lives is hugely determined by the expectations of the people around us,' followed by Thalia Wheatley, PhD from Dartmouth, a woman with short blonde hair, who adds, 'The brain helps us be the most social species on the planet,' both speaking directly to the camera in well-lit, modern indoor settings with large windows, their names and affiliations displayed on-screen.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with two women conversing on a sunlit urban boardwalk, followed by an earnest indoor discussion between two women. Daniela Schiller, PhD, from the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, then speaks directly to the camera in a softly lit office, stating, "A lot of our brains are devoted to understanding other people," and continues, "Our brain doesn't operate in isolation. We constantly learn, take, compare to other brains." Light, inquisitive background music plays throughout. Luke Chang, PhD, from Dartmouth, is then seen in a library-like room with bookshelves, speaking thoughtfully, stating, "Our brains have evolved to be able to effortlessly reason about other people," and adding that "emotions similarly have evolved as ways that guide our behavior." The scene shifts to a clinical setting where a woman with blonde hair appears distressed, touching her forehead, while a man looks on. A female narrator asks, "So how exactly do emotions and the emotions of others influence our brains?" accompanied by faint beeping sounds. A woman in a lab coat walks through a door with a red warning light into a controlled environment, where a researcher adjusts a white plate on a table. A laptop screen displays a trial interface showing "You earned $8 for this trial!" The narrator explains, "Neuroscientist Luke Chang studies how emotions like greed and guilt affect our decision-making." The MRI monitor displays a detailed sagittal brain scan, while a patient lies inside the MRI machine, viewed from a monitor labeled 'Magnet Bore.' A male voice instructs, "Hey Grace, we're gonna start up the scout," to which a female voice responds, "Okay." The male voice then prompts, "Go ahead and make your decision," and the female voice confirms, "Okay." The male voice adds, "You told her to go," and the female voice replies, "Yep, you can hit next," all accompanied by prominent clicking sounds. The female narrator asks, "So, what are you guys looking at here? What's this study about?" and the male voice, likely Luke Chang, explains, "So she's playing an investment game with another participant who's outside the scanner." The scene cuts back to Luke Chang as the narrator states, "Luke scans the brain..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a dimly lit office, where a woman with long dark hair and glasses, wearing a dark blazer, sits intently beside a man in a dark suit and light blue shirt, also wearing glasses, both looking at a screen. A female voice begins to explain how they study participants while they play a game from behavioral economics called 'the trust game.' The scene transitions to a man in a dark suit counting a stack of $50 bills, then handing a single bill to a woman in a black coat, visually illustrating the game's mechanics. A male voice describes this as 'a cooperative game where one person has some money and they can choose to invest any amount to that money in their partner.' A hand is then shown placing money into a brown leather wallet with a zipper. The female voice continues, explaining that 'that investment grows,' and the participant 'has to decide' if 'they could be greedy and keep all the money, or they could be generous and give some of the investment back.' This is followed by a handshake between two individuals against a cityscape, symbolizing the cooperative aspect. The scene shifts to hands typing on a keyboard, then to the man in the dark suit, now in a well-lit room with bookshelves, speaking directly to the camera. The male voice elaborates, stating, 'We've always been really interested in why do people return the money when they don't have to,' suggesting that 'guilt provides one plausible mechanism that might be driving their behavior to act cooperatively in this game.' The video then cuts to a computer monitor displaying a detailed sagittal MRI scan of a human brain, labeled 'Dartmouth College - PBS' and 'SD00217.' The woman and man from the opening are shown again in an office, engaged in conversation, with the woman gesturing as she speaks. The female voice returns, discussing how 'you're balancing and making these decisions between getting that kind of dopamine reward hit from being a little selfish versus being balanced by those feelings of maybe guilt when you're not cooperating or helping somebody else out.' The camera then focuses on a large screen showing a live, grainy, blue-tinted view of a patient’s head and torso inside an MRI machine, labeled 'Magnet Bore.' The final shot returns to the woman and man in the office, now looking at a computer monitor displaying multiple brain MRI scans in cross-section, with the woman pointing at the screen as they discuss the images. Throughout the segment, a subtle, investigative background music plays, and the PBS NOVA logo is consistently displayed in the top left corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a computer monitor displaying a sagittal MRI scan of a human brain, as a female narrator explains how "brain scans reveal which parts of the brain are most active when someone is feeling guilt." The scene then cuts to the Man in Suit, a male neuroscientist, who elaborates, stating that "those regions ended up being something called the insula," which generates "signals about having this gut feeling that maybe this isn't a good idea or I'd feel really bad if I did that," enabling decisions "to avoid harming someone else." An animated black-and-white brain diagram appears, highlighting the 'INSULA' in yellow, as the female narrator introduces an analogy: "Luke likes to think of it, kind of like a thermometer and a thermostat," accompanied by a new, slightly mysterious background music. A wide shot of a snow-covered college campus with a prominent brick building and steeple is shown, then cuts back to the Man in Suit, who continues, "If you try to think about how a thermostat might be mapped onto the brain, one region might be more like the thermometer detecting the ambient temperature in the room." A hand adjusts a mechanical thermostat dial from 70 to 80 degrees, followed by an animated thermometer graphic connecting to the insula in the brain diagram, as the narrator clarifies, "When it comes to reading the room, our brain's thermometer seems to be the insula." The Man in Suit reappears, explaining, "But all that information needs to go somewhere else and be integrated with other types of information." A digital thermostat is then adjusted from 80 to 70 degrees, and the animated brain diagram returns, showing a connection from the insula to the 'VENTRAL MEDIAL PREFRONTAL CORTEX.' The narrator identifies this as "our brain's thermostat. A region located inside the prefrontal cortex that processes our emotions and helps regulate our behavior." The segment concludes with another close-up of the mechanical thermostat being turned, as the narrator begins to say, "And while your thermostat..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a hand adjusting a beige mechanical thermostat from 70 to 60 degrees, as a female voice explains how such control usually helps manage emotions, then asks, 'What would happen if it went out?' This transitions to a stylized black-and-white brain diagram highlighting the 'VENTRAL MEDIAL PREFRONTAL CORTEX' in red and the 'INSULA' in yellow, with a glowing connection, illustrating neural pathways. A sepia-toned historical photograph of a man in formal 19th-century attire, holding a long iron rod and showing an injury above his eye, appears as a male voice introduces him as 'a famous patient named Phineas Gage.' A silhouette animation then vividly narrates the accident, showing a man on a railroad track being struck by a tamping iron that pierces and exits his skull, with the female narrator detailing how Gage, a railroad foreman, was tamping down gunpowder which ignited, sending the rod 'through his eye up through his brain, taking out a big patch of his brain in the process.' The woman in a dark turtleneck then speaks directly to the camera, before a detailed anatomical illustration of a skull with the iron rod passing through and a cross-section of the brain with the rod’s path is shown. The historical photograph of Gage reappears, now framed in ornate gold, as the woman continues, explaining that 'at first, people thought, well, this is a miracle. This man has been unscathed from this accident. He had memory, he had language, he had motor control.' A sepia photograph of a 19th-century railroad construction site provides context. The historical photograph of Gage returns, followed by the woman, as the male voice interjects, describing how Gage's 'life fell apart, he had a hard time holding a job, he lost all of his friends, and he really just struggled.' The female narrator concludes, detailing how 'his personality made him more fitful, irreverent, more profane. He was cursing a lot, lewd behavior, so he had sort of no filter.'

This animation smoothly transitions to a real, weathered human skull with visible cracks and a hole, mirroring the illustration, while the same female voice narrates that over a hundred years later, neuroscientists mapped the brain regions harmed in the horrific accident, specifically the prefrontal cortex, which might account for social struggles and inability to regulate emotions. The Woman with Short Blonde Hair then appears on screen, speaking directly to the camera, emphasizing that this was a key moment in neuroscience history when people realized that personality and morality are also located in the brain. The video cuts to a man in a dark suit seated in an office, then to a woman with long dark hair and glasses, who speaks expressively, asking what extra information is gained about how emotions impact decisions. The final shot shows a woman with curly dark hair lying in bed, eyes closed, appearing to be waking up or struggling to sleep. Throughout the segment, a subtle, reflective background music plays.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the white PBS NOVA logo in the top left corner. Two women on a white couch in a sunlit living room cover their mouths, laughing or reacting with surprise, as a male voice discusses scientific work on emotion and decision-making, questioning if emotions always lead to worse decisions. The scene shifts to a man in a blue and white plaid shirt, sitting outdoors on a stone bench, initially contemplative, then covering his face in distress, as the same male voice explains how emotions can help us make better decisions when considering self-interest and not harming others. An upbeat, light orchestral music interlude with chime sounds accompanies two women joyfully laughing and embracing in a modern kitchen, holding coffee mugs, with a pineapple-shaped kettle visible. Next, a man in glasses and a dark blazer speaks in an office, while a female voice begins to state, "We are in fact the company that we keep." This is followed by a view of a balcony with a black metal railing and a close-up of a hand with a silver bracelet gripping it, then a woman with long dark hair looking out a window, as the female voice continues, explaining that "other people bring out parts of us and strengthen us in particular ways." A woman with short blonde hair in a dark turtleneck then speaks directly to the camera with a distinct accent, detailing "how you make decisions, how you behave, how you think about yourself," and how "all of these processes we develop by mimicking and interacting and synchronizing with other brains." The scene transitions to a woman with long blonde hair in a light blue shirt speaking to the camera, as the first female voice returns, emphasizing that "social life and social contact is an incredibly important part of what our brain processes." This is visually reinforced by a bustling city street at sunset with pedestrians, followed by two men conversing in a dimly lit restaurant, and a group of women laughing outdoors under string lights. Finally, a woman with curly hair and glasses speaks expressively, gesturing in front of a large window with a city view, as the first female voice concludes, stating that "our brains are in detail influenced by every experience we have, every moment, every sentence, every image." The segment ends with a stylized 3D animation of a brain overlaid with glowing orange neural filaments.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a stylized, monochromatic 3D animation of a human brain composed of interconnected dots and lines, symbolizing neural networks, set against a light gray background, with the PBS NOVA logo visible in the top left. A female voice states that something "changes your brain," and that "certain experiences are so profound, so extreme that they can impact brain biology from one generation to the next." The scene transitions to a close-up of a computer monitor displaying a vibrant, colorful 3D brain scan with glowing neural activity, viewed through a blurred foreground object. The camera then shifts to Bianca Jones Marlin, a woman with short, silver hair, wearing an orange sleeveless top, seated at a lab workstation, focused on a computer screen displaying a detailed brain scan, with a microscope and lab equipment in the background. The narrator identifies her, stating, "Neuroscientist Bianca Jones Marlin is studying how your ancestors' experiences might control how your brain is wired today." Bianca then turns to the camera, now wearing a bright orange blazer, and speaks directly to the viewer, with on-screen text identifying her as 'BIANCA JONES MARLIN, PHD, COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY.' She explains, "We ask how trauma affects the brain, how trauma affects the body, and really how trauma affects generations. People in the world suffer from traumatic events and these traumatic events aren't just a one-time change on their brain and their body, it actually continues for seemingly their lifetime." The scene cuts to a vintage, slightly faded photograph of a young child with a woman and a man, suggesting a personal family moment, as the narrator adds, "Bianca's research is inspired by her upbringing." The video then returns to Bianca in a home kitchen with warm lighting, where she is cooking at the stove while two other people stand nearby, engaging in conversation amidst wooden cabinets and a microwave. Bianca continues, "My parents, my biological parents, were also foster parents. So I had foster siblings and adopted siblings growing up. Only now as a scientist I realize that that motivates a lot of the questions that I ask. How do we understand what happens when kids are born into trauma?"

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, in a warm, modern kitchen with wooden cabinets and natural light, cooking while two others stand nearby, creating a casual, familial atmosphere, as she states, "...and optimize what we do have for better generations." A gentle, reflective background music track begins, and the PBS NOVA logo is consistently displayed in the top left. The scene abruptly transitions to grainy, black-and-white archival footage of a European cityscape, showing a grand building with a clock tower and a bridge over a river, as a female narrator explains, "One insight comes from an event during World War II." This historical context is further elaborated by Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, who appears in present-day interview segments in a professional setting, wearing a bright orange blazer over a mustard-yellow top, speaking expressively with hand gestures. She explains, "At the end of World War II, the Netherlands were cut off from food by Nazi troops because they decided to protest with other countries. And during this period of time, it created a man-made famine. There was starvation, death, there was trauma." This dialogue overlays the archival footage, which continues to show wartime imagery: soldiers in helmets marching, a woman in a white coat carrying a child, and civilians, including children, queuing for food from large metal pots, with close-ups of a young child’s face conveying vulnerability and distress, highlighting hardship and scarcity. The female narrator then continues, stating, "Not only did those who suffered during the famine experience health problems, but some of their children and even their grandchildren had metabolic issues," while Bianca Jones Marlin continues to speak expressively. Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, then poses a key question, "So people began to ask, how does an experience of a parent, of a grandparent, change offspring?" The narrator elaborates, "Researchers began to discover that your environment and your experiences can change the way your genes are activated in your body and in your brain," as Bianca Jones Marlin continues to speak expressively. The segment concludes with a modern lab scene where a man in a white lab coat works at a computer, and a woman in a white lab coat, wearing glasses and a purple top, is seen putting on a surgical mask and cap, suggesting a transition to a scientific or medical context. Over this, Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, emphasizes, "It's not like you get your genes and it's set in stone, they're constantly changing based on the environment." The reflective background music continues throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, seated at a lab workstation, intently observing a computer monitor displaying a vibrant, multi-colored fluorescent microscopy image of a tissue section. With a clear, articulate voice, she explains that "almonds [are] important in this environment, we need more cells like you." As the image on the screen is overlaid onto her face, she continues, "Mice grew more cells that responded to the smell of almond." The scene transitions to close-ups of the microscopic image, highlighting specific neurons with green fluorescent tags, which she identifies as "neurons... cells that can respond to the almond smell." She then points out "red dots [which] are cells that were born after the presentation of odor and shock," and a specific "red and green cell" that was "born after the presentation of almond and shock, that also responds to almond." This specific cell, she explains, is what they "want to look at to see what information is inside because we see more of these after the odor and shock pairing." The scene cuts to live footage of tiny, hairless, pink newborn mice nestled in wood shavings, wriggling and huddled together, as a calm, authoritative narrator states, "Remarkably, these changes were actually passed down to the next generation." This is followed by a close-up of an adult black mouse eating in a transparent enclosure, and a researcher’s hands in orange nitrile gloves gently holding a black and a white mouse, illustrating the research subjects. The narrator clarifies that "The offspring, the kids of the parents that were shocked with odor, were born with more cells that express the almond receptor." The segment concludes with Bianca Jones Marlin, now in a red blazer over her mustard-yellow top, speaking directly to the camera in an interview setting with animated and engaged expression, as the narrator concludes, "Which means there's a memory that somehow is maintained..." Throughout, a subtle, inquisitive background music underscores the scientific narrative.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a woman with long, wavy brown hair, wearing a green jacket and patterned scarf, writing thoughtfully in a yellow notebook at a dimly lit table, as a professional female voice (later identified as Heather Berlin) describes a "magnificent ability" to reflect on our own minds, which can also "lead us astray." This transitions to a glowing 3D animated brain model with blue and red neural activity, while the same voice continues discussing memories, plans, and feelings of agency over actions. Anil Seth, PhD, a bald man in a black long-sleeve shirt, then speaks expressively in a studio, explaining that science tells us "these things aren't necessarily bound together" and that "different aspects of the self can be manipulated or even taken away altogether." Next, a woman with short dark hair and glasses reviews papers on a gray couch in a bright living room, while Heather Berlin's voice concludes, "And yet we feel like we have control, like we have agency, right?" The background music, which has been soft and contemplative, then becomes more dramatic and electronic with a distinct "whoosh" sound as a woman with long reddish-brown hair walks confidently down a modern office hallway. Finally, Uri Maoz, PhD, a man with short graying hair and glasses, speaks directly to the camera in a softly lit setting, defining "an agent" as "somebody that is the author of their own story," before continuing to explain that "most of what's happening in our brain, we are not conscious of," and that this realization "gets you starting to think, wait a minute, you know, is really everything under my..." as the segment ends.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the white PBS NOVA logo visible in the top left corner as a female narrator's voice introduces neuroscientist Uri Maoz, explaining he is "putting our sense of control to the test" and questioning its origin and function, accompanied by background music. Visually, the Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair, wearing a blue velvet blazer and dark pants, walks confidently down a modern office hallway with beige carpeting and glass partitions, passing a potted plant before approaching and entering a conference room through a wooden door marked 'URI MAOZ'. Inside, Uri Maoz, PhD, a man with short graying hair and glasses, wearing a dark blazer over a black shirt, is already present, standing near a large wooden table with black office chairs, holding a red folder. The woman greets him with an "Ah, there you are" and a "Hello," to which Uri responds with a "Hello" of his own, then warmly thanks her for joining "agentically and out of your own volition." The woman replies "Of course." The camera briefly blurs and refocuses on the conference table, which holds a notebook, markers, sticky notes, and a yellow block on a chair, as Uri, gesturing, instructs her, "Before we start, let me give you this envelope," adding, "Please, don't let anybody touch it and don't look inside, but we'll need it for later on." The woman acknowledges with "Mhm" and "Okay," then "For later. Okay, I'm gonna..." The narrator (the woman's voice) clarifies that Uri is trying to show her "how my sense of control isn't always what it seems" by questioning her "ability to choose" using a "magic trick." Uri then asks, "So, where would you like to sit?" prompting the woman to ask, "Where would I like to...?" Uri emphasizes, "It's really up to you. You have a choice," to which she responds, "I have a choice?" She decides, "All right, so I'm gonna sit here," and Uri confirms, "You're gonna sit over there. Okay." He then adds, "How about just before you sit down if you don't mind..." as the minute concludes, maintaining a professional and collaborative atmosphere.

Okay." as she sits across from him at the large wooden conference table. They discuss the card, with the woman questioning, "So then that one obviously says the same thing, right?" Uri, in a calm, instructional voice, replies, "Um, no. Let's check and see what this one says. This one says..." The woman exclaims, "Oh, come on," then asks, "So I'm that predictable? The woman's voice continues, reflecting on the experience, "I really don't know how he did that. I'm not totally convinced, but I'm starting to question, how do I know when I have made a decision." Uri then hands her a book, stating, "If I may, let me give you as a present, a book... this is yours and I would just ask you to leaf through it and find a word that has some meaning for you." She opens the book to dense academic text, then confirms, "All right, I got it." Uri asks, "Can you tell me what the word is?" to which she replies, "Representation." He instructs her to "Please write the word down, representation, and you know, just stick that sticky note somewhere in that page," as she picks up a small white card. She acknowledges, "Okay. Okay. All right." Her voice then adds, "We'll come back to that later." Soft, contemplative background music begins as her voice continues, "But for now, I'm starting to see how choice and..."
