segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a hand precisely adjusting a knob on a medical control unit, its digital display showing '69.0' and '0', as light, inquisitive background music begins. A female narrator explains that to understand the brain's control, she observed a trial designed by postdoctoral researcher Alice Wong. The scene then shifts to a dimly lit laboratory where Alice Wong, a a woman in a gray blazer and glasses, carefully positions a white, circular transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) coil over the head of a seated volunteer from the lab, Tomasz, who wears a white cap and dark jacket. On-screen text labels the procedure as 'TRANSCRANIAL MAGNETIC STIMULATION' and 'TMS', as the narrator further explains that TMS generates a strong magnetic field to send signals to the brain. The video cuts to an interview with a man in a dark suit and glasses, who, gesturing toward his head, elaborates that stimulating the motor cortex—the part of the brain controlling fingers—is like pulling a string, causing the finger to move. Returning to the lab, the TMS coil is activated, and Tomasz’s hand, connected to electrodes, twitches slightly, confirming the motor response. The narrator confirms that researchers can make his finger jump involuntarily. Alice Wong intently observes a computer screen displaying a waveform graph, adjusting equipment, and is heard speaking, "We're going to be locating the..." followed by a click, emphasizing the precision and scientific rigor of the experiment, all while the PBS NOVA logo remains in the top left corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Tamasha's hand, resting on a gray towel, with white electrodes attached to his palm and connected by thin wires, indicating a physiological monitoring setup. Tamasha, wearing a dark zip-up jacket over a gray turtleneck and a white cap with a circular device, is seated in a dimly lit lab environment, appearing focused. A woman in a gray sweater, likely one of the researchers Alice or Uri, adjusts equipment behind him, while a female voice explains, "of your motor cortex that moves one of your fingers." She then asks, "How about that?", to which Tamasha responds, "That works. That was a pinky movement up." The woman confirms, "Okay." The camera pans to a computer monitor displaying a red waveform graph, which spikes intermittently, suggesting real-time neural or muscle activity recording. A narrator explains that sometimes Tamasha is asked to move his finger on his own, and the woman asks, "Could you replicate the movement if that you felt?", to which Tamasha replies, "Something like this." The narrator elaborates that by recording the small electrical signals from Tamasha's brain to his finger muscles, Alice and Uri can pinpoint the exact moment his brain initiates a movement, almost 50 milliseconds before he actually moves, effectively predicting his movements. Tamasha’s concentrated eyes are shown in a tight close-up as the monitor then displays a questionnaire. The narrator sets the scene: "So now a sense of agency is about to be put to the test." The woman reads the first question aloud, "Who initiated the movement?", and Tamasha promptly answers, "That was me." She continues, "How much agency did you feel over the movement?", and Tamasha replies, "Quite a lot," with the woman confirming, "Full agency. Okay." An overhead shot reveals the full lab setup: Tamasha seated with his hand on the apparatus, the monitor in front of him, and two researchers observing and recording data in the technical, controlled environment, with dim lighting and various electronic devices, including a white machine with green and blue indicator lights, emphasizing a neuroscience experiment focused on motor control and agency perception.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an overhead shot of a dimly lit laboratory, revealing a participant, identified by a female narrator as Tomash, seated at a table. Tomash, a man with a beard wearing a white cap with a TMS device mounted on his head, a dark zip-up jacket over a gray turtleneck, and a 'CHAPEL HILL' logo, is engaged in a motor control experiment. He presses illuminated purple buttons on a wooden board with his right hand, while electrodes are attached to his palm, connected by thin wires. A synthesized female computer voice from the monitor asks, "Who initiated the movement?", to which Tomash responds in a male voice, "I don't know." The monitor, displaying a blue circle, later shows a questionnaire interface with prompts like "How much agency did you feel over the movement?", which the computer voice also asks. Tomash replies, "I would say some agency?" A woman in a gray blazer and glasses stands beside him, adjusting equipment and observing the monitor, while another woman in a gray sweater takes notes. The lab is equipped with a white medical control unit with green and blue indicator lights, and a gray towel is placed under Tomash’s arm for comfort. The camera alternates between close-ups of Tomash’s focused expression, his hand pressing buttons, and the electrodes. The narrator explains that in some instances, just as Tomash decides to move his finger, researchers use the magnetic field to make his finger move. When the computer voice again asks, "Who initiated the movement?", Tomash states, "I really don't know." After the computer says "Okay," it asks, "Uh, how much agency did you feel over the movement?", and Tomash replies, "A little bit." The narrator continues, noting that even when Tomash decided to move his finger, he didn't always feel in control, especially when he responds "No agency at all" to the computer's repeated question about agency. The overall atmosphere is clinical and focused, emphasizing the precision of the experiment, with the PBS NOVA logo remaining in the top left corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a close-up of a participant’s hand, palm up, revealing white adhesive electrodes connected by thin wires, resting on a soft gray towel. A male voice, identified as Uri Maoz, PhD, immediately begins explaining the experiment, stating, "Yet, we intervened with the TMS," and recounting the participant's perception: "Tomash said, 'That wasn't me. I didn't initiate the movement.' It was the computer." The scene shifts to the participant, a man with a beard wearing a white cap with a TMS coil mounted on it, seated in a dimly lit lab environment, focused on pressing illuminated purple buttons on a wooden board. Uri Maoz continues, detailing how the participant "almost never said that it was him" who initiated the movement. An unseen female voice then interjects, asking, "So, what do you think is going on there? How is this happening?" Uri Maoz responds, explaining that humans typically feel like "the authors of our actions," but with "just a little bit of messing around, it tends to fall apart." The female voice elaborates, noting that "our sense of self, our memories, our sense of agency... they're fragile and they can be manipulated under the right circumstances." The camera alternates between close-ups of the participant’s concentrated face, the electrodes on his hand, and the attentive expressions of two researchers, including Uri Maoz and a woman in a blue velvet blazer, observing the experiment. Uri Maoz, later shown in a well-lit conference room, speaking expressively and gesturing with his hands, further explains that "everything has to align for you to feel this sense of agency," describing how feedback from finger movement is "incorporated with whatever is happening in the brain to create the movement." The lab setting features technical equipment with green and blue indicator lights, maintaining an atmosphere of scientific observation and controlled experimentation. Uri Maoz concludes by stating that while "in everyday life we are in control," this experiment suggests otherwise. The segment ends with a wide shot of the collaborative lab setup, showing the participant, researchers, and equipment in context.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a white transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) coil positioned over a participant, a man in a white cap and a 'CHAPEL HILL' jacket, as a male voice states, "relinquish control." The scene widens to show him seated at a table, pressing illuminated purple buttons, while a woman in a gray blazer adjusts the TMS device and another woman in a gray sweater takes notes in a dimly lit lab. Thalia Wheatley, PhD, from Dartmouth, then appears, speaking directly to the camera, explaining that "Like states of consciousness, there are levels of agency, ways it can be manipulated, and even taken away," and that "most of our brain activity is unconscious." Back in the lab, the participant continues his motor task, and a monitor displays a questionnaire asking, "Who initiated the first movement?" A female voice asks this question, to which the participant's male voice responds, "That was me." Anil Seth, PhD, from the University of Sussex, then speaks from a studio, explaining how "we sometimes misinterpret our experience of voluntary action" as "a little bit retrospective," where "the brain looks at what the body did and figures out if that makes sense as an act of its own free will." Light, slightly mysterious background music begins as Thalia Wheatley's voice narrates, "After the agency experiment, we had more important matters to attend to." The video transitions to a conference room where Uri Maoz, PhD, is seen conversing with a woman with long reddish-brown hair, identified as Heather. Uri Maoz's voice asks Heather, "when you came in, I gave you an envelope, right?" to which Heather's voice confirms, "Yes." He continues, "Nobody touched it but you?" and she replies, "No." Uri Maoz then explains, "later on, I gave you that book," which she later examines along with a small white card, while the table holds a yellow block, markers, and sticky notes, emphasizing the shift from clinical experimentation to academic discussion.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a woman’s hands holding an open book with a yellow sticky note, as her clear voice states, "the word was representation." The camera then reveals the Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair, wearing a blue velvet blazer and glasses, seated across from Uri Maoz, PhD, who is dressed in a dark blazer. Uri, speaking in a calm, deeper voice, repeats "Representation" and instructs her to put the book aside and take out an envelope. As she picks up a bright red envelope, Uri asks her to open it and see what’s inside. With a hint of apprehension, the woman remarks, "Oh, this is one of these things that's gonna freak me out, right?" Uri encourages her with "Let's see," and she whispers, "Getting chills," as the sound of paper rustling accompanies her opening the envelope. She pulls out a small white card with 'Representation' printed on it, showing it to Uri with a surprised smile. Her voice rises in disbelief, exclaiming "Come on! No way! No, seriously! That's really freaky." Uri, amused, asks, "So you're in control, right?" to which she responds with amazement, "I don't know how you did that. That is really weird. What do I do now? I don't know where to... what do I do with that?" The scene transitions to a deck of playing cards being spread out on the table, with the woman pointing to specific cards. A female narrator (the same woman) then explains, "Uri's magic acts are tricks. Sleights of hand and misdirection. But when I saw what was written on the card, I have to admit, I wondered if my choices mattered at all." Uri's voice then calmly states, "Let me do this," as the segment continues, maintaining the PBS NOVA logo in the top left corner throughout this collaborative and intellectually engaging interaction.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment begins with a close-up of a wooden table where a hand places a blue-backed playing card next to a fanned-out deck, as a female voice (Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair) states, "a decision is made," then asks, "How does she do that?" The Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair, in a blue velvet blazer and glasses, is then seen seated at the table, conversing with Uri Maoz, PhD, as she explains, "Your sense of agency or control also has to do with feedback you get after the decision, physical, social, and emotional." Uri Maoz, PhD, then speaks, describing, "I think of agency as a sense, so there is a sense of agency that sometimes can get disrupted perhaps, just like you have a sense of sight or smell and so on." The scene cuts to the Man in Dark Suit, gesturing expressively in a sunlit, modern interior with large windows, as he states, "Sometimes you have visual illusions." He continues, "It's similar with a sense of agency. I can manipulate your sense of agency, but that doesn't mean that we never have a sense of agency." A close-up shows a hand with a silver ring touching a black metal chain, followed by a blurred, fast-moving shot of a busy city street. The Man in Dark Suit reappears, but the female voice (Woman with Long Reddish-Brown Hair) is heard saying, "Your brain is a meaning maker machine. And creating a sense of agency is one of the ways it makes meaning out of your daily life." A vibrant 3D animation of a neural network with glowing blue filaments appears as a different female voice (Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD) states, "There is no way in which I can operate without understanding what is happening and why I'm doing it." The scene shifts to a young woman in a gray hoodie writing in a notebook by a snowy window, with on-screen text promoting the PBS App, then a silhouette of a hiker walking uphill toward a bright sun, while the female voice (Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD) continues, "It's the filling in of the blanks that is necessary in some ways for survival, to give meaning, to make sense of the cause and effect of things." Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD, is then seen speaking expressively in a softly lit room, wearing a yellow top and colorful glasses, as a male voice (Uri Maoz, PhD) interjects, "Perhaps we have..." The segment concludes with an overhead view of a crowded plaza with blurred people, all accompanied by a subtle, thoughtful background music track.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dynamic overhead shot of a bustling plaza, showcasing a diverse crowd and urban energy, while the PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner. Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD, from the University of Chicago and Argonne National Laboratory, then appears in a close-up, speaking directly to the camera from a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls, wearing a dark floral shirt and glasses, as he discusses the 'feeling of consciousness,' 'sense of agency,' and the 'rewards or failures' of decision-making. This transitions to a close-up of hands writing in a notebook, overlaid with a glowing 3D neural network animation, before Heather Berlin, PhD, MPH, is seen in front of a wooden bookshelf, wearing a black blazer over a sparkly top, explaining that while 'parts of the brain' allow us to feel like the 'author of your own life,' that's 'only part of the story.' A series of quick cuts follows: Nancy Kanwisher, PhD, in a blue t-shirt and blue glasses, states that 'each of our minds' is 'composed of lots of different pieces'; Rebecca Saxe, PhD, with curly light brown hair and glasses, speaks about the 'illusion that there's a single person inside our skulls'; Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, in a red blazer with short silver hair, explains 'how environment and how traumatic events change people'; and Mahzarin R. Banaji, PhD, with short gray hair and colorful striped glasses, elaborates that 'our brains are in detail influenced by the expectations of the people around us.' The visual returns to Heather Berlin, who adds that 'most of our brain activity is unconscious.' The video then transitions to an outdoor park scene, lush with greenery and flowers, where a man in a dark suit plays a golden saxophone, with close-ups of his hands and face as he performs bluesy music. Over this music, a female voice (likely Heather Berlin) describes 'some situations where letting go of conscious control can have amazing results,' before the saxophonist himself explains that 'when you're playing the blues, you have this kind of well-known musical structure, this template.'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Charles Limb, MD, from the University of California, San Francisco, speaking in a modern, well-lit office, wearing a dark blazer over a gray shirt and glasses, discussing using something as a “launchpad for improvisation, for innovation, and for new ideas.” The PBS NOVA logo is visible in the top left. A female narrator introduces Charles Limb as a neuroscientist trying to understand how our brain operates when we are truly creative. The scene transitions to a close-up of a computer monitor displaying detailed brain MRI scans. The camera then cuts to educator and freestyle rapper Chris Emden, wearing a stylish navy blue double-breasted suit, white fedora, round glasses, a striped shirt, and a red flower lapel pin, walking confidently through a sunlit outdoor campus area, gesturing expressively as he raps, “It's gonna be ill in the MRI. This Dr. E freestyle.” The narrator explains Charles is using a scanner to peer into Chris Emden's brain, as Chris continues rapping, “I wonder if I'm going to say anything, I'm freestyling, profiling, still wilding, it's gonna be ill.” The scene shifts to a dimly lit MRI lab where Chris Emden, now in a striped shirt and jeans, is assisted by a technician in a white lab coat into a large Siemens MRI machine, which emits a low hum. Charles Limb observes from a control room, asking Chris, “Ready for me?” to which Chris replies, “Yeah, I'm ready.” Charles instructs Chris to “keep your head still during the entire thing and try not to move your feet or your hands at all during the rapping,” and Chris confirms, “Alright, do the best I can.” A woman with long dark hair joins Charles in the control room to monitor the scan on a laptop. The narrator explains Charles asks Chris to perform a memorized piece. Charles clarifies, “Now that memory means you're going to do the memorized lyrics the way you originally wrote them, okay?” Chris responds, “Okay.” Charles then prompts, “Memory,” and Chris begins rapping, “I'm a physicist, lyricist, spit in this ridiculousness, a witness...” as the MRI hum continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a dimly lit control room with a close-up of Charles Limb, MD, wearing glasses and a gray button-down shirt, intensely focused on a computer screen. The camera pulls back to reveal Limb and a woman with long reddish-brown hair seated at a desk, monitoring a patient undergoing an MRI scan in an adjacent room, visible through a window in a large Siemens MRI machine. A female narrator explains that the patient is given a prompt to "improvise, to create a new original piece on the spot," a concept further elaborated by Limb's male voice stating the patient "doesn't know what's coming" and these are his "cues." As Limb gestures while speaking to the woman, the computer monitor displays a grid of MRI brain scans. The scene transitions to a close-up of hands playing a piano keyboard, with a translucent 3D brain model superimposed over the keys, visually linking musical activity with neural processing. Simultaneously, the patient's rhythmic spoken word performance begins, with lyrics directly referencing his situation: "physicist, lyricist," and humorously noting, "the MRI machine won't let me do it." A female voice, possibly the woman in the control room, remarks, "This is good." The female narrator then poses the question, "So what does improvisation or spontaneous creativity look like in the brain?" Limb's male voice then begins to explain their findings, mentioning the "prefrontal cortex" and its link to "effortful self-monitoring," as the segment concludes. The white PBS NOVA logo with a brain graphic remains in the top left corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of hands playing a piano, overlaid with a translucent 3D brain model that pulses in sync with the background music, as a male voice, belonging to the Man with Glasses, explains how highly trained professional musicians deactivate certain brain areas when improvising. The scene transitions to a dimly lit office where Heather Berlin, PhD, MPH, is seen gesturing expressively while conversing with the Man with Glasses. Her voice elaborates on how 'letting go' and 'decreasing activation in the prefrontal cortex' can lead to greater control in life. The Man with Glasses interjects, warning that being 'too self-conscious' hinders performance, and that attempting to exert 'conscious control' can actually degrade it. Heather Berlin then asks if this principle applies to any activity, giving the example of a professional tennis player, which is visually represented by a woman in a pink shirt serving a tennis ball on an outdoor court at dusk. The Man with Glasses agrees, then illustrates this further with the example of 'free throw shooters' who, despite high accuracy, might 'choke' when offered a large sum of money for a single shot, injecting conscious control into a subconscious process. This narrative is interspersed with visuals of a saxophonist playing with focused intensity, a patient lying inside an MRI machine, a soccer player kicking a ball at sunset, and a basketball player successfully shooting a hoop, all underscored by the continuous background music. The segment concludes with a stylized black-and-white animation of a human head, its skull dissolving to reveal a detailed brain, symbolizing the intricate neural processes discussed.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a stylized black-and-white 3D brain model, its prefrontal cortex highlighted in yellow and labeled, rotating against a dark background, as a female voiceover explains how "parts of your brain that are usually in control can get in your way," and how the "prefrontal cortex, the decision-maker, can make you overthink something you've done a thousand times," accompanied by a subtle, low ambient hum. The scene transitions to a dimly lit lab where a man in a gray shirt presses a green button on a control panel, observed by a woman beside him, while a male voice begins a rhythmic, spoken-word performance, saying "Freestyle, stay. Yes, you want me to stay. Relax, but I will never play." The camera then cuts to this man, in a gray blazer and glasses, speaking expressively in a sunlit office with striped shadows across the table, continuing his thought in a conversational tone, stating, "Every human being is creative," and elaborating that "Life is not scripted. And so, no matter who you are in this world, you're doing things that are unplanned." A quick montage follows, showing a construction worker on a ladder, a chef tossing food in a wok over a flame, a nighttime cityscape, a man in a black puffer jacket and beanie talking on a phone in a snowy forest, and a young man in a blue t-shirt engrossed in his phone. During this, the female voiceover returns, stating, "All day long, we're balancing forces that push us around, even if we're not aware of them," and continuing as the montage shows a group of friends laughing outdoors, a man in a blue checkered shirt touching his temple, a close-up of a hand writing with a red pen, and a man with curly gray hair and glasses speaking in a dimly lit room with exposed brick, concluding her thought by mentioning "From past trauma to the emotions of others and all the hidden forces affecting your brain." The final shot is a woman with long dark hair and glasses, wearing a plaid shirt, adjusting her glasses against a blue twilight sky, appearing contemplative, as a new male voice with a calm, reflective tone begins to speak, saying, "I'd like to believe that I am in charge of my life, that I am the agent of my life, that I actually can control my..." before the audio cuts off. The PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a young woman with curly hair and glasses, wearing a plaid shirt, looking thoughtfully into the distance against a twilight sky, as a man with curly gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark floral shirt, speaks expressively in a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls about how his understanding of 'emotions, my abilities, my desires' has evolved, realizing earlier beliefs were 'probably not true.' The scene transitions to a man with a bald head and a beard, wearing a black shirt, speaking directly to the camera in a studio, explaining how 'we can be influenced by our social networks, by our culture, by our genetics, by our development, by our childhood.' Following this, two dancers in elaborate traditional Thai costumes perform synchronized gestures on stage under dramatic lighting, then a child rides a scooter down a leaf-strewn path in a park. Intricate mechanical gears and watch components are shown in close-up, accompanied by the distinct sound of gears turning and ticking. A woman with short gray hair and blue glasses, wearing a blue t-shirt, speaks expressively in a modern, well-lit room, describing how 'Your brain is a complicated collection of these intricate parts, many of which you have no awareness of,' and how 'they all work together in a delicate dance to create your perception of you.' This is followed by a stylized 3D animation of a brain, with the temporal lobe highlighted in red and the cerebellum in yellow, overlaid on the mechanical gears, then the parietal lobe in green. A woman with curly light brown hair and glasses, wearing a dark sleeveless top, speaks in front of a city view, stating, 'The brain is who you are. It's really different than any other organ in that sense.' Next, a woman with long dark hair, wearing a white floral dress, speaks with an accent, explaining that 'every experience, every thought, every memory, every sensation has its origin in the brain.' segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri, MD, PhD, speaking expressively in a dimly lit room with exposed brick walls, his calm, authoritative voice explaining how neuronal connections create an individual. Upbeat, slightly mysterious background music begins as the scene transitions to a woman with long dark hair in a black blazer, conversing in a lab, while an unidentified female voice reflects, "Years of studying the brain have humbled me." A split-screen then shows a sleep monitoring screen with polygraph readouts and a night-vision view of a person sleeping, who suddenly sits up and emits a sharp scream, prompting an unidentified male voice to exclaim, "Whoa! He looks scared!" The scene shifts to an operating room where a surgeon in blue scrubs and a woman in a surgical cap and mask focus on a patient, with a close-up of a Masimo medical monitor displaying vital signs like a heart rate of 85 bpm and brain wave activity, as the female voice continues, "You can't control everything that makes you who you are. But the unconscious you is still you." Rapid cuts show a rock climber on a cliff face, a woman stretching on a bridge, and another woman lifting a kettlebell in a gym, while the female voice explains, "The vast majority of the brain's work is happening outside conscious awareness." A woman with long dark hair sits at a desk with a computer, followed by a close-up of hands playing a guitar, as Narayanan 'Bobby' Kasthuri's voice advises, "If you try to overcontrol some things, you actually will decrease your performance," and then, "You have to let go of conscious self-monitoring to just kind of like go with the flow." Bianca Jones Marlin, PhD, speaks expressively in a modern indoor setting, followed by a woman with short blonde hair in a dark turtleneck speaking in a softly lit room, as a second distinct female voice comments, "It could be scary to say and scary to hear," and then, "We are not just our own." A 3D brain model with colorful regions rotates against a dark background, followed by a woman with curly hair and glasses speaking outdoors with a glowing, translucent brain model superimposed beside her, as a third distinct female voice declares, "We are all multifaceted, multi-dimensional people." The initial female voice returns, concluding, "And by becoming more aware of the unconscious processes in your own brain, you can become more aware of what drives you, and what you ultimately can control," with the background music swelling, all while the PBS NOVA logo remains consistently in the top left corner.
